elements.sent-2411-001: The optimal design of multi-target rendezvous and flyby missions presents significant challenges due to their inherent combination of traditional spacecraft trajectory optimization with high-dimensional combinatorial problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: This often necessitates the use of large-scale global search techniques, which are computationally expensive, or the use of simplified approximations that may yield suboptimal results.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address these issues, a nested-loop approach is proposed, where the problem is divided into separate combinatorial and optimal control problems.
elements.sent-2411-004: The combinatorial problem is formulated and solved using Binary Integer Programming (BIP) with a fixed rendezvous time schedule, whilst the optimal control problem is handled by adaptive-mesh Sequential Convex Programming (SCP), which additionally optimizes the time schedule.
elements.sent-2411-005: By iterating these processes in a nested-loop structure, the approach can efficiently find high-quality solutions.
elements.sent-2411-006: This method is applied to the Global Trajectory Optimization Competition 12 (GTOC 12) problem, leading to the creation of several new best-known solutions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Introduction Multi-target mission design represents a growing trend within the fields of space exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-008: Traditionally, spacecraft trajectory design has focused on missions with a single primary target or objective \citep{hallPioneer10111974, reinhardGiottoEncounterComet1986, schmidtMarsExpressESA2003a .}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, recent missions, such as the recently launched JUICE \citep{grassetJUpiterICyMoons2013 and Lucy \citep{englanderTrajectoryDesignLucy2019 missions, have begun to adopt the concept of targeting multiple primary objectives.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In the literature, this class of problems is often exemplified by asteroid tour design \citep{izzoAutomatedAsteroidSelection2007, longStochasticMissionExploration2024, dicarloLowthrustTourMain2018 , where a single spacecraft performs rendezvous or flybys with multiple asteroids along its trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The design of such multi-target missions is important because because they typically offer significant reductions in the cost to visit each target when compared to sequences of single-target missions.
elements.sent-2411-012: Consequently, these problems have been frequently proposed in spacecraft optimization challenges, such as the Global Trajectory Optimization Competition (GTOC) \citep{izzo1stACTGlobal2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The design requirements of multi-target missions introduce a considerable increase in complexity over single-target missions.
elements.sent-2411-014: This is primarily because, in addition to optimizing the trajectory, the selection and sequencing of the targets must be integrated into the optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-015: Such problems are frequently encoded as Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming (MINLP) \citep{schlueterNonlinearMixedInteger2012 problems, which are also known as Hybrid Optimal Control Programming (HOCP) \citep{rossHybridOptimalControl2005 problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In these formulations, a set of integer variables is used to define the sequence of targets to visit, whilst continuous variables handle the optimization of the control parameters.
elements.sent-2411-017: Although significant efforts within the literature have been put into directly solving problems in this encoding, their solutions are often difficult to obtain and are computationally expensive when compared to alternative solution methodologies \citep{dambrosioMixedIntegerNonlinear2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: A common strategy to mitigate the difficulties of solving MINLP problems is to partition the combinatorial ordering problem (the integer variables) from the optimal control problem (the continuous variables) and solve them in a sequential manner.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, this approach introduces challenges due to the strong coupling often encountered between the two problems.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, in asteroid tour design, the optimal sequence of targets directly influences the optimal control profile and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-021: One method that attempts to address this issue is to employ a nested optimization process \citep{englanderAutomatedMissionPlanning2012 , which iteratively alternates between solving the combinatorial and optimal control problems, rather than handling them in strict sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, developing efficient solution methods for both the combinatorial and optimal control problems is key to enabling an effective nested optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-023: The combinatorial ordering problem closely resembles the well-known Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) \citep{lawlerTravelingSalesmanProblem1985 , although with additional complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Consequently, a graph-based transcription is commonly employed, which allows for the use of a variety of search techniques that make use of this transcription.
elements.sent-2411-025: Depth-first and breath-first search methods \citep{cormenIntroductionAlgorithmsFourth2022 systematically enumerate the entire search space, ensuring that once completed, they will identify the optimal combination along with all feasible solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the complete enumeration of the search space is often intractable, which can be partially addressed by branch and bound \citep{lawlerBranchandBoundMethodsSurvey1966 and its extensions, notably branch and cut \citep{padbergBranchCutAlgorithmResolution1991 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: These methods utilize a similar tree structure to depth- and breath-first search methods but prune the search space by eliminating branches when suboptimality or infeasibility can be established.
elements.sent-2411-028: Many leading commercial solvers, including Gurobi and CPLEX, implement advanced variations of this approach to tackle Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) problems \citep{anandComparativeAnalysisOptimization2017 , of which Binary Integer Programming (BIP) is a subset.}
elements.sent-2411-029: As the size of the combinatorial problem grows, the additional complexity often makes it challenging (and sometimes intractable) to work with the complete problem.
elements.sent-2411-030: This leads to a clear preference towards incomplete search methods.
elements.sent-2411-031: Branch and bound (and branch and cut) methods can be employed with early termination, where a comparison between primal and dual objectives helps to quantify suboptimality relative to the global solution \citep{lawlerBranchandBoundMethodsSurvey1966 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Beam search is also widely used; it follows a breadth-first graph search strategy but limits computational effort by ranking branches at each level and expanding only a select number of the best options.
elements.sent-2411-033: This method is notable in that has been successfully applied across many iterations of GTOC \citep{zhangGTOC11Results2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Dynamic programming can also be used, which is advantageous in that it directly leverages the optimal substructure property, allowing combinatorial problems to be split into smaller problems for which the optimal solution is the combination \citep{bellomeTrajectoryDesignMultiTarget2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: For the most difficult combinatorial problems, stochastic algorithms are often preferred due to their flexibility and robustness across large classes of problems \citep{blumMetaheuristicsCombinatorialOptimization2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Stochastic algorithms include Differential Evolution (DE), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Simulated Annealing (SA), Genetic Algorithms (GA), and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), all of which have been applied with varying degrees of success to spacecraft tour design problems \citep{conwaySpacecraftTrajectoryOptimization2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The optimal control problem is also often challenging to solve and is generally considered to be a complex task.
elements.sent-2411-038: It is typically addressed through methodologies from the field of optimal control, which are well-established in the literature and have been applied across a wide range of engineering problems.
elements.sent-2411-039: Methods to solve optimal control problems can generally be classified into direct, indirect, and heuristic methods \citep{chaiReviewOptimizationTechniques2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Sequential Convex Programming (SCP) is a direct optimization method that has been widely applied to various aerospace applications, including launch vehicle ascent guidance \citep{benedikterConvexApproachThreeDimensional2021 , powered descent guidance \citep{kwonSequentialConvexProgramming2021 , and low-thrust multi-revolution interplanetary transfers \citep{hofmannComputationalGuidanceLowThrust2023a .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Numerous modifications to the core SCP algorithm have been proposed to improve performance, including those that explore different discretization strategies.
elements.sent-2411-042: Notably, adaptive-mesh SCP \citep{kumagaiAdaptiveMeshSequentialConvex2024 incorporates the time variable within the dynamical linearization and is therefore introduced into the convex approximation of the NLP.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This adaptation allows for the mesh generated from the discretization (based on the direct transcription) to adjust dynamically, enabling superior optimality to be achieved with fewer transcription segments.
elements.sent-2411-044: However, for spacecraft optimal control problems, this approach presents a significant drawback: the introduction of the time variable can substantially degrade the quality of the dynamical linearization \citep{kumagaiAdaptiveMeshSequentialConvex2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: As a result, the use of trust regions and additional iterations of SCP becomes necessary.
elements.sent-2411-046: This paper introduces a nested-loop approach for solving multi-target spacecraft trajectory optimization problems using a combination of BIP and SCP.
elements.sent-2411-047: Firstly, the combinatorial aspect of the problem is solved using a BIP with a similar form to minimum-cost flow problems \citep{ahujaNetworkFlowsTheory1993 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: An underlying graph structure is developed where the rendezvous of specific targets is represented by nodes and the intermediate transfers by arcs.
elements.sent-2411-049: The objective is to minimize transfer costs, which are computed in a discrete manner by assuming a fixed time schedule and approximating the cost via Lambert transfers.
elements.sent-2411-050: The BIP is constructed by introducing binary optimization variables for each arc, along with the necessary constraints, to ensure a single continuous trajectory is selected in the desired form.
elements.sent-2411-051: Following the solution to the combinatorial problem, the optimal control profile and rendezvous timing are simultaneously found using an adaptive-mesh SCP formulation, which has been substantially extended to permit many trajectories to be optimized at once.
elements.sent-2411-052: Once the optimal rendezvous timing has been found, the combinatorial aspect of the problem is resolved, and the process is iterated until convergence is achieved, completing the nested-loop approach.
elements.sent-2411-053: The proposed approach forms a central component of our strategy to identify a range of high-quality post-competition solutions for the problem proposed in the 12th edition of GTOC \citep{gtoc12ProblemDescription .}
elements.sent-2411-054: This problem entails finding an optimal campaign of low-thrust ship trajectories, where each ship visits a sequence of asteroids to deploy miners and collect mined mass from the asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-055: The number of ships in a campaign is dependent on the total amount of mined mass, which, in turn, depends on the duration mining each asteroid.
elements.sent-2411-056: A trade-off must be struck between time, fuel consumption, and the number of asteroids visited.
elements.sent-2411-057: The winning solution by JPL presented a campaign of 35 ships that collectively mined mass from 313 asteroids, resulting in 626 rendezvous events.
elements.sent-2411-058: This clearly establishes the substantial scale necessary for high-quality solutions to the problem.

elements.sent-2411-059: The 12th edition of GTOC (GTOC 12) \citep{gtoc12ProblemDescription presented a challenging multi-target spacecraft trajectory design problem titled {\it sustainable asteroid mining .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: This problem required designing a campaign of low-thrust spacecraft trajectories (ships) throughout the asteroid belt with the objective to mine as much mass as possible.
elements.sent-2411-061: A brief overview of the problem is provided below.
elements.sent-2411-062: A catalog of 60,000 asteroids is provided as potential targets, resulting in an extremely large combinatorial problem size for potential trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-063: Important parameters related to the spacecraft and dynamics of the problem are detailed in Table~\ref{gtoc_parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-064: It is worth noting that the spacecraft engine parameters are somewhat unrealistic compared to current technology - particularly the 4000 $s$ $I_{\text{sp }$ and corresponding thrust of 0.6 $N$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: However, these parameters were designed to represent the future possibilities at the mission start time of 64328 MJD (January 1, 2035).
elements.sent-2411-066: The objective of the problem is to maximize the total mass mined from the asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-067: Each ship must launch from Earth within a specified timeframe and can rendezvous with asteroids, where miners can be deployed.
elements.sent-2411-068: After some time, a ship can rendezvous with the same asteroid again to collect the mined mass before returning to Earth.
elements.sent-2411-069: A ship only contributes to the objective once it has returned to Earth with the collected mass.
elements.sent-2411-070: An example of the events throughout a ship trajectory is demonstrated in Figure~\ref{gtoc12_explainer .}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this example, a ship deploys miners and collects mined mass from three asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-072: It is important to note that the order of deployment and collection events do not need to follow the same sequence.
elements.sent-2411-073: Crucially, it is permitted for a coordinated campaign of ships to be launched, and the careful selection of ship trajectories that do not conflict becomes a problem.
elements.sent-2411-074: There is no requirement for deployment and collection events to be performed by the same ship.
elements.sent-2411-075: For instance, a miner can be deployed by one ship and the mined mass collected by another.
elements.sent-2411-076: The use of such strategies was a key factor that distinguished teams' solutions within the competition.
elements.sent-2411-077: Therefore, ships that both deploy and collect from the same asteroids are referred to as {\it self-cleaning (similar to the trajectory seen in Figure~\ref{gtoc12_explainer ), and those that employ collaborative strategies are referred to as {\it mixed .}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The number of ships allowed within a campaign depends on the average mined mass collected per ship, making ships that rendezvous faster and visit more asteroids highly advantageous.
elements.sent-2411-079: This behavior is described by the formula: \begin{equation}\label{number_ships} N \leq \min \left( 100, 2 e^{0.004 \bar{M}} \right)\end{equation} where $N$ is the number of ships within a campaign and $\bar{M}$ is the average mined mass returned to Earth by each ship.
elements.sent-2411-080: Consequently, small increases in average mined mass per ship can significantly impact the overall campaign objective, by enabling the inclusion of whole additional ships into the campaign.
elements.sent-2411-081: The dynamics of the ships follow Keplerian motion with low-thrust control.
elements.sent-2411-082: With the state $\mathbf{x =[\mathbf{r , \mathbf{v , m]$ and control $\mathbf{u =[\mathbf{T ]$ the dynamical equations are as follows: \begin{equation}\label{dynamical_equation} \dot{x} = f(x, u) = \left\{{l} \dot{r} = v \dot{v} = -\frac{\mu}{r^3} r + \frac{T_{max}}{m} T \dot{m} = -\frac{T_{max}}{I_{sp} g_0} T \right.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{dynamical_equation} \dot{x} = f(x, u) = \left\{{l} \dot{r} = v \dot{v} = -\frac{\mu}{r^3} r + \frac{T_{max}}{m} T \dot{m} = -\frac{T_{max}}{I_{sp} g_0} T \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, the amount of mined mass is directly proportional to the amount of time that a miner has been deployed at the asteroid: \begin{equation}\label{mined_mass} m_{mined} = m_{rate} \left( t_{collection} - t_{deployment} \right)\end{equation} where $t_{\text{deployment }$ and $ t_{\text{collection }$ represent the time of miner deployment and mass collection, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: As a result, an alternative expression for the objective of a campaign can be expressed as maximizing the total time between miner deployments and collections.
elements.sent-2411-085: Methodology This section outlines the proposed approach for finding high-quality solutions to multi-target spacecraft trajectory design problems and then, separately, the assembly of many of these solutions into a coordinated campaign.
elements.sent-2411-086: As a preface, whilst many of the constraints and objectives are specifically tailored to the GTOC 12 problem, this approach is highly applicable beyond this context and can be easily generalized to a wide range of spacecraft trajectory design problems.
elements.sent-2411-087: A central element to this approach is the use of a nested-loop structure, which is used to facilitate the transfer of updated information between the different sub-problems.
elements.sent-2411-088: By iteratively solving computationally tractable sub-problems rather than approaching the full problem at once, high-quality solutions can be obtained from the problem in an efficient manner.
elements.sent-2411-089: For this process, the sub-problems are constructed from the natural division of the full problem into what are labelled the combinatorial problem and the optimal control/rendezvous time problem.

elements.sent-2411-090: Figure~\ref{nested_flow outlines the overall structure of the solution process.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The main inputs to the process are an asteroid subset restricting the search to a limited number of asteroids out of the full data set and an initial estimate of the times for asteroid rendezvous to occur.
elements.sent-2411-092: These inputs are then transferred into a BIP to solve the combinatorial problem and determine the optimal sequence of asteroid rendezvous for each spacecraft.
elements.sent-2411-093: Subsequently, this sequence is transferred to the optimal control/rendezvous time problem, which is solved using SCP.
elements.sent-2411-094: The output of the SCP step is the optimal control profile and the corresponding optimal rendezvous times.
elements.sent-2411-095: Since the optimal rendezvous times are likely to differ from the initial estimates (or even those from previous iterations), they are passed back to the BIP, and the process is repeated to form the nested-loop until no further improvements in the solution are found.
elements.sent-2411-096: Clearly, the assumptions that are made during the division of the full problem into sub-problems will significantly impact the optimality of the process.
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, many of these decisions have been made with the GTOC 12 problem in mind.
elements.sent-2411-098: The most impactful assumption is the restriction of the search space through the use of asteroid subsets.
elements.sent-2411-099: Given that the GTOC 12 problem presents 60,000 possible targets for rendezvous, the combinatorial problem easily becomes intractable in its full form.
elements.sent-2411-100: However, from this full set of possible targets, only a small number are good candidates for rendezvous.
elements.sent-2411-101: These tend to be well-connected and easily accessible from other asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-102: To isolate groups of these asteroids, a pruning and grouping process is applied, forming the {\it subsets .}
elements.sent-2411-103: This process evaluates the average Lambert transfer costs over the problem timeframe and is detailed in \citep{armellinGTOC12Results2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: As a result, smaller groups of asteroids ranging in size from approximately 10 to 300 asteroids are created, making the combinatorial problem more tractable.
elements.sent-2411-105: Additionally, an initial guess must be made for the rendezvous times.
elements.sent-2411-106: The selection of this initial guess is problem-specific and can be informed by the problem constraints.
elements.sent-2411-107: However, the initial guess does not need to be extremely accurate due to the optimization step in the SCP.
elements.sent-2411-108: For the GTOC 12 problem, our analysis determined that a rendezvous spacing of approximately 150-250 days would be appropriate for high-quality solutions \citep{armellinGTOC12Results2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-109: This insight is used to derive an initial guess of the rendezvous times.
elements.sent-2411-110: Moreover, the start and end times of the rendezvous sequence are approximated by averaging the time of arrival and departure for optimal Lambert transfers from and to Earth for all asteroids within each subset.
elements.sent-2411-111: Finally, an approximation is made to the asteroid-to-asteroid transfer costs by using the $\Delta v$ calculated via Lambert's problem, rather than the true mass cost.
elements.sent-2411-112: This approach offers two key advantages: solutions to Lambert's problem are very fast to calculate, and this estimate is independent of the spacecraft mass, which can only be approximated at each stage because it depends on the spacecraft's trajectory history.
elements.sent-2411-113: Because of this approximation, the output from the combinatorial problem may not perfectly match the true optimal ordering.
elements.sent-2411-114: Therefore, to improve the exploration of the algorithm, several of the best combinatorial solutions are refined in each stage of the nested-loop process.

elements.sent-2411-115: As a demonstration example of the methodology, we introduce a restricted case of a GTOC 12 trajectory optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-116: This exemplar case uses a highly restricted subset of 5 asteroids, which will enable a detailed analysis of the behavior of each stage of the process.
elements.sent-2411-117: The orbital elements of the asteroids in this subset, listed in Table~\ref{restricted_parameters , are all clearly very similar.}
elements.sent-2411-118: In the full problem, similar characteristics for the asteroids within a subset would be expected, as having similar orbital elements would permit the optimal transfer costs to be kept low and, subsequently, for the transfer times to be reduced.
elements.sent-2411-119: The initial rendezvous time schedule is used for the example problem is outlined in Figure~\ref{transfer_times_restricted .}
elements.sent-2411-120: A total of 3 asteroid deployment and collection events are designed, and in this case if a feasible trajectory is found that meets the GTOC 12 constraints, it would yield a maximum of 302.59 kg of mined mass.
elements.sent-2411-121: This time schedule is calculated with a fixed rendezvous spacing of 175 days, and the initial and final transfer times are approximated based on optimal Lambert transfer durations.
elements.sent-2411-122: Notably, the waiting time between the deployment and collection phases spans almost 10 years, with a duration of 3334 days.
elements.sent-2411-123: Binary Integer Programming BIP is used to solve the combinatorial ordering problem and provide guarantees on the optimality of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-124: The main challenge lies in finding an appropriate transcription of the problem that allows for efficient solving.
elements.sent-2411-125: These types of problems share a very similar structure to well-known routing problems, such as the traveling salesman \citep{lawlerTravelingSalesmanProblem1985 and optimal flow \citep{ahujaNetworkFlowsTheory1993 problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Since both of these problems are commonly interpreted using a graph theory approach, we develop a similar framework to describe this formulation.
elements.sent-2411-127: It is important to note that this formulation does not account for the initial and terminal transfers required for a valid GTOC 12 trajectory (i.e., the transfers from Earth to the asteroids and from the asteroids to Earth).
elements.sent-2411-128: Each subset contains a variety of asteroids with similar characteristics, meaning that these initial and terminal transfers would approximately have equal cost across different asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-129: Thus, their choice should only have minimal impact on the optimality of the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-130: During our development and testing, this was tested with a modified formulation that included the cost of these transfers, but because no significant differences in solution quality were observed, they are omitted from the final formulations to simplify the problem.
elements.sent-2411-131: Using graph theory terminology, a node is created for each potential rendezvous target in our subset, while the transfers between them are represented by arcs.
elements.sent-2411-132: Unfortunately, as in many spaceflight optimization problems, the cost to transfer between targets can vary significantly over time and often in an unpredictable manner.
elements.sent-2411-133: This marks a significant difference from standard traveling salesman-like problems.
elements.sent-2411-134: To work around this limitation, the continuous transfer costs are translated into a fixed rendezvous time schedule.
elements.sent-2411-135: As a consequence, transfers in the BIP can only start and end at predetermined times, which makes the complete enumeration of all possible transfer arcs tractable.
elements.sent-2411-136: To account for this change, a separate set of nodes is introduced for each of the fixed rendezvous times, with directed arcs connecting them to represent the transfers between asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-137: The costs of these arcs are therefore fixed since the rendezvous times are predetermined.
elements.sent-2411-138: In summary, starting from a set of possible rendezvous targets, each target is represented by a requisite node for each rendezvous time.
elements.sent-2411-139: Between each of these sets of nodes are directed arcs, which each represent a transfer between asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-140: The cost of each of these arcs is fixed due to the known time schedule at which each rendezvous occurs.
elements.sent-2411-141: Therefore, a natural BIP formulation would be to introduce a binary variable for each possible arc.
elements.sent-2411-142: In the GTOC 12 problem, the deployment and collection rendezvous events need to be distinguished from one another.
elements.sent-2411-143: To achieve this within the BIP formulation, separate binary variables are introduced: $d_{i, j, k \in [0, 1]$ for deployment events and $c_{i, j, k \in [0, 1]$ for collection events.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Additionally, the variable $m_{i, j \in [0, 1]$ is defined to represent the intermediate transfer phase between the deployment and collection phases of a mission.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In the notation for these variables, the first subscript ($i$) represents the departure target, the second subscript ($j$) is the arrival target, and the third subscript ($k$) denotes the stage in the rendezvous schedule.
elements.sent-2411-146: When a subscript index is demarked with end, it indicates the selection of the last valid index for that specific subscript.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{figures/bip_pruned.png} \caption{An example of the graph structure of the BIP variables for GTOC 12 problems. Edges in bold remain after the pruning step.} \label{bip_connected}
elements.sent-2411-147: For example, to create the structure for the deployment and collection of 3 miners in a single mission from the example subset of size 5, $d_{i, j, k}$ and $c_{i, j, k}$ would have indices $i$ and $j$ from 1 to 5 (representing the choice of asteroid) and $k$ from 1 to 2 (representing the 2 directed arcs that connect 3 rendezvous events).
elements.sent-2411-148: Similarly, the $m_{i, j}$ would have $i$ and $j$ from 1 to 5.
elements.sent-2411-149: A visual representation of this graph structure is shown in Figure~\ref{bip_connected .}
elements.sent-2411-150: The visualization of this graph structure immediately highlights the potential for optimizations by removing unnecessary arcs, corresponding to the elimination of variables within the BIP formulation.
elements.sent-2411-151: In order to help facilitate this process, the $d$, $m$, and $c$ variable arrays are initialized as sparse matrices, where only variables that meet certain criteria are included into the problem formulation.
elements.sent-2411-152: Firstly, arcs connecting to the same target during the deployment or collection phases of the mission are not feasible and can be removed, formally $d_{i, j, k}$ where $i=j$ and $c_{i, j, k}$ where $i=j$.
elements.sent-2411-153: However, for the intermediate variables $m_{i, j}$, these transfers are permitted since it is often optimal to wait at the same target between the deployment and collection phases.
elements.sent-2411-154: Additionally, a cost-based pruning step removes arcs that are unlikely to be used due to their high predicted costs.
elements.sent-2411-155: For the purposes of the GTOC 12 problem, this criterion was typically set to when the calculated Lambert $\Delta v$ of a specific transfer exceeded $6$-$10$ km/s.
elements.sent-2411-156: This criterion is applied across all arcs within the formulation.
elements.sent-2411-157: After applying these pruning steps, the resultant graph structure is illustrated by the bold arcs in Figure~\ref{bip_connected .}
elements.sent-2411-158: The total number of binary variables decreased from 125 in the original formulation to 61 in the pruned formulation.
elements.sent-2411-159: It is important to note that in some cases, this pruning can be too aggressive and lead to problem infeasibility; this can be easily addressed by increasing the cutoff for the $\Delta v$ threshold.
elements.sent-2411-160: Several constraints are required within the BIP formulation in order to produce feasible rendezvous sequences for the GTOC 12 problem (or even rendezvous sequence problems in general).
elements.sent-2411-161: The precise formulations of these constraints are very important, as they should only restrict transfers that are genuinely infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-162: Each of these constraints is detailed in the following paragraphs.
elements.sent-2411-163: Collectively, these constraints guarantee that across the whole trajectory, only one arc is selected at each stage: \begin{equation}\label{bip_single_per_stage} \forall k: \sum_{i, j} d_{i, j, k} = 1 \sum_{i, j} m_{i, j} = 1 \forall k: \sum_{i, j} c_{i, j, k} = 1\end{equation} (arc departs if an arc arrives) If an arc arrives at a specific asteroid, another arc must depart from that same asteroid in the next stage of the trajectory.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{bip_single_per_stage} \forall k: \sum_{i, j} d_{i, j, k} = 1 \sum_{i, j} m_{i, j} = 1 \forall k: \sum_{i, j} c_{i, j, k} = 1\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-164: This ensures the continuity of the rendezvous sequence: \begin{equation}\label{bip_continuity} \forall j, \forall k: \sum_{i} d_{i, j, k} = \sum_{i} d_{j, i, k+1} \forall j, \forall k: \sum_{i} c_{i, j, k} = \sum_{i} c_{j, i, k+1}\end{equation} (deployment/collection once per phase) Deployments and collections must not occur more than once at the same asteroid.

elements.sent-2411-187: A clear limitation of the self-cleaning BIP formulation is its inability to optimize a critical aspect of the GTOC 12 problem: collaboration among different mining ships to develop a mixed strategy.
elements.sent-2411-188: Many of the highest-scoring solutions in the competition, including the winning strategy by JPL, heavily leveraged this to produce better-quality campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-189: To address this, several modifications to the self-cleaning BIP formulation are introduced to enable the optimization of mixed solutions using a similar methodology.
elements.sent-2411-190: This process involves duplicating the self-cleaning formulation for each mixed ship.
elements.sent-2411-191: Rather than treating these as separate BIP problems, all variables and constraints are integrated into a single BIP and solved simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-192: To account for the additional sets of variables required, the variables pertaining to each of these graph structures are denoted with a superscript index, such as $d^{\,l _{i, j, k}$.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Then, some of the constraints for each mixed ship need to be modified in order to generate feasible solutions.
elements.sent-2411-194: Firstly, while the constraints for selecting a single arc per stage \eqref{bip_single_per_stage , making sure an arc departs if one arrives \eqref{bip_continuity , and selecting the correct intermediate variable \eqref{bip_intermediate are unchanged, they now apply to each ship individually (i.e., one copy of each constraint for each superscript index).}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Then, some of the other constraints need to be further altered, which is detailed in the following paragraphs.
elements.sent-2411-196: The only restriction is that the total number of deployment events must equal the total number of collection events.
elements.sent-2411-197: This fits naturally into the formulation, the only change is that the third index for deployment and collection on each ship may no longer increment to the same value.
elements.sent-2411-198: Care must be taken that the intermediate variable selection constraint \eqref{bip_intermediate is correctly implemented, considering that the end index may vary between ships.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Finally, the objective for the mixed ship BIP formulation becomes: \begin{equation}\label{bip_objective_multi} J = \sum_{i, j, k, l} \alpha^{\,l}_{i, j, k} d^{\,l}_{i, j, k} + \sum_{i, j, l} \beta^{\,l}_{i, j} m^{\,l}_{i, j} + \sum_{i, j, k, l} \gamma^{\,l}_{i, j, k} c^{\,l}_{i, j, k}\end{equation} In this formulation, instead of solely considering the cost of a single self-cleaning ship trajectory, the goal is to minimize the combined cost of all trajectories.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{bip_objective_multi} J = \sum_{i, j, k, l} \alpha^{\,l}_{i, j, k} d^{\,l}_{i, j, k} + \sum_{i, j, l} \beta^{\,l}_{i, j} m^{\,l}_{i, j} + \sum_{i, j, k, l} \gamma^{\,l}_{i, j, k} c^{\,l}_{i, j, k}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-210: Sequential Convex Programming To solve for the optimal control profile and the optimal rendezvous time schedule, an adaptive-mesh SCP is used \citep{kumagaiAdaptiveMeshSequentialConvex2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-211: In contrast to typical implementations of SCP for spacecraft trajectory optimization problems, this method incorporates the time variables into the optimization process, enabling for the the mesh discretization to be optimized simultaneously with the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-212: To accommodate the highly multi-target trajectories that are present in the GTOC 12 problem, these methods have been extended to support the simultaneous optimization of multiple rendezvous events throughout the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-213: It is then further extended to support the optimization of multiple ship trajectories simultaneously in a manner similar to the BIP.
elements.sent-2411-214: Firstly, the formulation of the SCP problem for a single ship trajectory is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-215: Each individual ship trajectory consists of multiple rendezvous events.
elements.sent-2411-216: In the GTOC 12 problem, some of these events involve the deployment of miners, while others pertain to the collection of mined mass.
elements.sent-2411-217: Between each successive event, there exists a trajectory arc on which the optimal control profile must be determined; this is referred to as a {\it leg of a trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Each leg is discretized into many individual parts over time as in a direct method, and these parts are referred to as {\it segments .}
elements.sent-2411-219: For the GTOC 12 problem, the number of segments in a leg is determined based on a predefined segment timespan, which is chosen prior to the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-220: For instance, a 201-day leg with 5-day segments would have a total of 40 5-day segments culminating with a terminal 1-day segment.
elements.sent-2411-221: Similar to the subscripts used in the BIP formulation, the first subscript for each variable denotes the segment, while the second subscript indicates the leg of the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-222: When a subscript index is marked with end, this signifies taking the last valid index for that specific subscript.
elements.sent-2411-223: Due to the incorporation of the time variable into the adaptive-mesh SCP, this discretization primarily serves as an initial guide for the optimization process because the timespan of each segment is selected during the optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-224: This parameter acts as a scaling factor for the timespan of each individual segment timespan and is optimized during the SCP process.
elements.sent-2411-225: Consequently, the exact timespans of the segments may vary between segments, allowing the optimization to adapt the trajectory segmentation and potentially identify better quality solutions.
elements.sent-2411-226: As a result, without a fixed integrality constraint across all segments, it becomes evident that the rendezvous times could change to enhance optimality.
elements.sent-2411-227: This flexibility, which is enabled by including the segment timespans into the optimizatioon, is crucial to obtaining solutions that are optimal not only in terms of control profile but also regarding their choice of rendezvous time schedule.

elements.sent-2411-268: Trust regions are introduced as simple trust regions that decrease linearly in size as the SCP algorithm progresses.
elements.sent-2411-269: The trust region constraints for the state and time are separated and take the following form: \begin{equation}\label{trust_regions} \forall {i}, \forall {j}: -\epsilon_1 \leq \left[{c} r_{i, j} - \mathbf{\bar{r}}_{i, j} v_{i, j} - \mathbf{\bar{v}}_{i, j}  \right] \leq \epsilon_1 \forall {i}, \forall {j}: -\epsilon_2 \leq \Delta t_{i, j} / \bar{\Delta t}_{i, j} - 1 \leq \epsilon_2\end{equation} A variety of values for the initial size of the trust regions were tested, but values of $\epsilon_1=2.5$ and $\epsilon_2=0.05$ tended to perform well for the problems tested.

elements.sent-2411-292: The SCP formulation can be adapted to optimize multiple ship trajectories simultaneously, which is essential when ships are interdependent and require joint optimization.
elements.sent-2411-293: Whilst self-cleaning ships in the GTOC 12 problem can be optimized independently because they do not impact each other, mixed ships present an example where simultaneous optimization becomes crucial.
elements.sent-2411-294: This is because different ships may separately handle the miner deployment and mass collection events for an individual asteroid, which couples their time variables.
elements.sent-2411-295: This coupling could influence each individual ship trajectory, so for mixed ship campaigns all ship trajectories must be optimized simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-296: As with the BIP, a separate SCP formulation is created for each ship, and all of the constraints and variables for each ship are integrated into a unified SCP formulation.
elements.sent-2411-297: To manage the additional sets of variables required, those pertaining to each ship are denoted with a superscript index, such as the time variable $t^{\,l _{i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-298: Most of the constraints from the single trajectory SCP (e.g., \eqref{dynamics_linear , \eqref{control_magnitude , \eqref{state_violation ) remain applicable but are now applied to each ship's trajectory independently.}}}

elements.sent-2411-310: The final step of the process to create a complete campaign of ship trajectories for the GTOC 12 problem is the selection process.
elements.sent-2411-311: After running the nested-loop process multiple times with different inputs, a large pool of possible ship trajectories is generated.
elements.sent-2411-312: The goal is to select the best combination of these ships whilst ensuring that there are no conflicts where multiple ships deploy or collect from the same asteroids.
elements.sent-2411-313: This selection process is achieved using a separate BIP formulation, which supports the optimization of self-cleaning, mixed, and hybrid campaigns containing both types of ships.
elements.sent-2411-314: Firstly, a binary variable $s_i \in [0, 1]$ is introduced to represent whether ship $i$ is selected.
elements.sent-2411-315: Several constraints are then enforced to ensure that only non-conflicting ships are chosen for the campaign.

elements.sent-2411-322: Results In this section, the process to construct high-quality self-cleaning and mixed strategies for GTOC 12 is outlined and demonstrated using the proposed methodology.
elements.sent-2411-323: Firstly, the construction of a high-quality self-cleaning solution is demonstrated from a restricted subset of asteroid targets.
elements.sent-2411-324: This is followed by a similar process for constructing a mixed set of solutions.
elements.sent-2411-325: Finally, 37-ship self-cleaning and 39-ship mixed campaigns are presented which have been primarily constructed through this methodology.
elements.sent-2411-326: These campaigns currently represent the best-known solutions to the GTOC 12 problem.
elements.sent-2411-327: All algorithms were written and implemented using version 1.11 of the Julia programming language and executed using an Intel Core i7-12700 processor with 32GB of RAM.

elements.sent-2411-395: Conclusions This work presented an approach for finding and optimizing multi-target spacecraft missions using a combination of Binary Integer Programming (BIP) and Sequential Convex Programming (SCP).
elements.sent-2411-396: A nested-loop structure was proposed, wherein the combinatorial ordering problem is first solved with a fixed rendezvous schedule, followed by solving the optimal control problem with a fixed combinatorial ordering.
elements.sent-2411-397: These steps are repeated in lockstep until convergence, forming the nested-loop structure.
elements.sent-2411-398: A graph-based BIP formulation was employed for the combinatorial ordering problem, and advanced BIP solvers were utilized to efficiently explore the solution space, identifying not only the best solution but a range of the top solutions.
elements.sent-2411-399: The optimal control problem is solved using an adaptive-mesh SCP, which calculates a new optimal control profile as well as a rendezvous schedule for a fixed combinatorial ordering.
elements.sent-2411-400: This method is applied in the context of the 12th Global Trajectory Optimization Competition (GTOC 12) problem, focusing on optimal multi-target missions throughout the asteroid belt.
elements.sent-2411-401: Applying the proposed adaptive-mesh SCP formulation directly to the top 10 teams' solutions of the GTOC 12 problem yielded substantial performance gains, demonstrating the superior capability of the proposed trajectory optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-402: Moreover, the combination of BIP and SCP within the nested-loop structure enabled the creation of a high-quality solution pool, leading to new best-know solutions, including 37-ship and 39-ship campaigns, all without resorting to high-performance computing facilities.
elements.sent-2411-403: One notable limitation of the approach is that the initial selection of rendezvous times still requires problem-specific tuning and insight to ensure convergence to a high-quality solution.
elements.sent-2411-404: However, the inclusion of more accurate estimates of transfer costs rather than Lambert estimates is expected to further improve the results and can be easily dropped into the methodology.
elements.sent-2411-405: Looking ahead, the flexibility of this algorithm should allow for future adaptation towards a wide range of multi-target spacecraft missions.

elements.sent-2411-409: The optimal design of multi-target rendezvous and flyby missions is challenging due to the combination of traditional spacecraft trajectory optimization and high-dimensional combinatorial problems.
elements.sent-2411-410: This often requires large-scale global search techniques or simplified approximations that rely on manual tuning to be performant.
elements.sent-2411-411: While global search techniques are typically computationally expensive, limiting their use in time- or cost-constrained scenarios, this work proposes a computationally efficient nested-loop approach.
elements.sent-2411-412: The problem is split into separate combinatorial and optimal control subproblems: the combinatorial problem is solved using Binary Integer Programming (BIP) with a fixed rendezvous time schedule, while the optimal control problem is handled with adaptive-mesh Sequential Convex Programming (SCP), which also optimizes the time schedule.
elements.sent-2411-413: When, applied to the Global Trajectory Optimization Competition 12 (GTOC 12) problem, this method results in several new best-known solutions.
elements.sent-2411-414: Traditionally, spacecraft trajectory design has focused on missions with a single primary target or objective \cite{hallPioneer10111974, reinhardGiottoEncounterComet1986, schmidtMarsExpressESA2003a .}
elements.sent-2411-415: However, recent missions, such as the recently launched JUICE \cite{grassetJUpiterICyMoons2013 and Lucy \cite{englanderTrajectoryDesignLucy2019 missions, have begun to adopt the concept of targeting multiple primary objectives.}}
elements.sent-2411-416: In the literature, this class of problems is often exemplified by asteroid tour design \cite{izzoAutomatedAsteroidSelection2007, longStochasticMissionExploration2024, dicarloLowthrustTourMain2018 , where a single spacecraft performs rendezvous or flybys with multiple asteroids along its trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-417: The design of such multi-target missions is important because they typically offer significant reductions in the cost to visit each target when compared to sequences of single-target missions.
elements.sent-2411-418: Consequently, these problems have been frequently proposed in spacecraft optimization challenges, such as the Global Trajectory Optimization Competition (GTOC) \cite{izzo1stACTGlobal2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-419: Such problems are frequently encoded as Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming (MINLP) \cite{schlueterNonlinearMixedInteger2012 problems, which are also known as Hybrid Optimal Control Programming (HOCP) \cite{rossHybridOptimalControl2005 problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-420: Although significant efforts within the literature have been put into directly solving problems in this encoding, their solutions are often difficult to obtain and are computationally expensive when compared to alternative solution methodologies \cite{dambrosioMixedIntegerNonlinear2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-421: One method that attempts to address this issue is to employ a nested optimization process \cite{englanderAutomatedMissionPlanning2012 , which iteratively alternates between solving the combinatorial and optimal control problems, rather than handling them in strict sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-422: The combinatorial ordering problem closely resembles the well-known Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) \cite{lawlerTravelingSalesmanProblem1985 , although with additional complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-423: Depth-first and breadth-first search methods \cite{cormenIntroductionAlgorithmsFourth2022 systematically enumerate the entire search space, ensuring that once completed, they will identify the optimal combination along with all feasible solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-424: However, the complete enumeration of the search space is often intractable, which can be partially addressed by branch and bound \cite{lawlerBranchandBoundMethodsSurvey1966 and its extensions, notably branch and cut \cite{padbergBranchCutAlgorithmResolution1991 .}}
elements.sent-2411-425: These methods utilize a similar tree structure to depth- and breadth-first search methods but prune the search space by eliminating branches when suboptimality or infeasibility can be established.
elements.sent-2411-426: Many leading commercial solvers, including Gurobi and CPLEX, implement advanced variations of this approach to tackle Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) problems \cite{anandComparativeAnalysisOptimization2017 , of which Binary Integer Programming (BIP) is a subset.}
elements.sent-2411-427: Branch and bound (and branch and cut) methods can be employed with early termination, where a comparison between primal and dual objectives helps to quantify suboptimality relative to the global solution \cite{lawlerBranchandBoundMethodsSurvey1966 .}
elements.sent-2411-428: This method is notable in that it has been successfully applied across many iterations of GTOC \cite{zhangGTOC11Results2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-429: Dynamic programming can also be used, which is advantageous in that it directly leverages the optimal substructure property, allowing combinatorial problems to be split into smaller problems for which the optimal solution is the combination \cite{bellomeTrajectoryDesignMultiTarget2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-430: For the most difficult combinatorial problems, stochastic algorithms are often preferred due to their flexibility and robustness across large classes of problems \cite{blumMetaheuristicsCombinatorialOptimization2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-431: Stochastic algorithms include Differential Evolution (DE), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Simulated Annealing (SA), Genetic Algorithms (GA), and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), all of which have been applied with varying degrees of success to spacecraft tour design problems \cite{conwaySpacecraftTrajectoryOptimization2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-432: Methods to solve optimal control problems can generally be classified into direct, indirect, and heuristic methods \cite{chaiReviewOptimizationTechniques2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-433: Sequential Convex Programming (SCP) is a direct optimization method that has been widely applied to various aerospace applications, including launch vehicle ascent guidance \cite{benedikterConvexApproachThreeDimensional2021 , powered descent guidance \cite{kwonSequentialConvexProgramming2021 , and low-thrust multi-revolution interplanetary transfers \cite{hofmannComputationalGuidanceLowThrust2023a .}}}

elements.sent-2411-454: Sequential Convex Programming To solve for the optimal control profile and the optimal rendezvous time schedule, an adaptive-mesh SCP is used \cite{kumagaiAdaptiveMeshSequentialConvex2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-455: In contrast to typical implementations of SCP for spacecraft trajectory optimization problems, this method incorporates the time variables into the optimization process, enabling the mesh discretization to be optimized simultaneously with the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-456: This parameter acts as a scaling factor for the timespan of each individual segment and is optimized during the SCP process.
elements.sent-2411-457: This flexibility, which is enabled by including the segment timespans into the optimization, is crucial to obtaining solutions that are optimal not only in terms of control profile but also regarding their choice of rendezvous time schedule.

elements.sent-2411-474: Conclusions This work presented a nested-loop optimization framework, combining Binary Integer Programming (BIP) and adaptive-mesh Sequential Convex Programming (SCP), which has been demonstrated to effectively address certain types of multi-target spacecraft trajectory problems.
elements.sent-2411-475: Applying this methodology to the 12th Global Trajectory Optimization Competition (GTOC 12) problem led to new best-known solutions, including 37-ship and 39-ship campaigns with mined mass per ship exceeding the performance of previously submitted solutions, thereby validating the effectiveness of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-476: Furthermore, the relative simplicity of the framework, particularly in its nested-loop structure, makes it both accessible and straightforward to implement.
elements.sent-2411-477: The application of adaptive-mesh SCP to previously submitted solutions for GTOC 12 identified significant suboptimalities in rendezvous timing, resulting in substantial performance gains.
elements.sent-2411-478: These improvements not only highlight the critical role of rendezvous time optimization to multi-target problems, but also help to enhance the effectiveness of the BIP process within the nested-loop framework.
elements.sent-2411-479: By refining the time schedule input to the BIP, the adaptive-mesh SCP enables the BIP to generate more accurate and efficient target selection, explaining how the two components mutually reinforce each other to enable superior results compared to existing solutions.
elements.sent-2411-480: While the need for problem-specific tuning to the initial rendezvous time schedule remains a limitation, this does not detract from the broader applicability of the framework, wherein a suitable approximation to rendezvous times is likely to already be known.
elements.sent-2411-481: The integration of more accurate transfer cost estimates into this framework would further reinforce its potential by improving the accuracy of the BIP approximations, and would be the object of future study.
